The faculty-to-student ratio in the institute is generally balanced, which helps students receive proper attention during lectures and practical sessions. Faculties maintain a good relationship with students and are mostly approachable. Students can easily ask questions after class or during doubt-clearing sessions, and many teachers are supportive when it comes to academic guidance and project work. Some of the faculties I liked include Mr. Patel and Ms. Shah because they explain topics clearly and use practical examples to make the subject easy to understand. They also encourage students to participate in discussions and are always ready to solve doubts. In my opinion, these teachers are among the best faculties because of their teaching style, patience, and dedication towards studentsâ€™ learning. However, there are a few faculties whose teaching style is not very engaging. For example, Mr. Akshay sometimes teaches very fast and does not always explain concepts in detail, which can make it difficult for some students to understand the topic fully. In terms of evaluation, the institute usually conducts internal tests, assignments, and final examinations each semester. The exams are moderately difficult. Students who study regularly and attend classes can pass easily, but those who do not prepare well may find them challenging. The course curriculum is relevant and designed to provide both theoretical knowledge and practical skills useful for future careers.

The yearly fee for my course is approximately 89, 225. This includes tuition fees, registration charges, development fees, examination fees, and other basic academic charges required by the college. Overall, the fee remains relatively stable compared to many private colleges. Students also have to pay a refundable caution deposit during admission and minor charges for activities, library and examinations during the course. Scholarships are available for students based on merit. Usually, the top three students for every course are given scholarships or fee concessions based on their academic performance. Some government scholarships are also available for students belonging to reserved categories such as SC, ST, EWS and OBC. The college itself provides scholarship to every Top 3 individuals (Yearly). These scholarships help reduce the financial burden for deserving students.

The placement cell is well-organised and communicates drive schedules well in advance. Mock interviews and aptitude sessions start from third year, which gives students adequate time to prepare. However, the variety of companies visiting is limited â€” most are IT service firms. Students from non-CS branches get significantly fewer opportunities. Most faculty members are knowledgeable and genuinely committed to teaching. Office hours are maintained and professors respond to queries fairly promptly. However, a few professors in core subjects still rely on decade-old notes and make no effort to update content. The inconsistency in teaching quality across faculty is frustrating.

Good companies do come but intake is limited. Companies come for both internship as well as placements. TNP cell is responsible for smooth functioning of these activities. Less than 7cgpa might result in not getting shortlisted to sit for a lot of companies. Curriculum is updated acc to the requirements of latest tech. Teachers are helpful. 75% mandatory attendance is required. Teachers emphasize more on practical and hands on learning rather than theories.

Reviews: Curriculums are good (it depends on depts) and they are updated and new electives/ cbcs are added thus updating curriculum and there are subject with heavy lab which don't have end sem instead lab and only internal which is not what other colleges have. they also have open book test for subjcts like programming languge so students can focus on learning the language instaed of memorizing. however as we are batch of a new regulation we are not aware of kind of questions that would come. however forthcoming batches won't be having this issue. unlike other collges only 2 internal exams and 1 semester is conducted. both the internal is given equal weightage ( having unit 1&2 and 3&4 for each exams) and retest is also conducted for internal however even if one exam not written they need to write all 4 unit incase of health issues etc. 50% internal and 50% external. so if having good internal can easlity score well in total. Placements at DTU are generally very good, with many reputed companies visiting the campus every year. Students receive opportunities in tech, consulting, and core sectors with competitive salary packages. The faculty in the college are well qualified and most have good know how of their core field. The fac to stud ratio is near 1:15 which helps in good class link and easy doubt clear. Most fac are easy to talk to and help stud with proj work, labs and exam prep. Some of the best fac in the dept were Dr Ravi Kumar, Prof Suresh and Dr Meena as they used clear teach ways, real life ex and gave good guide in proj work. They also gave time to stud out of class hours. A few fac were not liked by some stud as their teach style was more theory based and class talk was less clear, which made some topics hard to grasp. The course plan is well set and has both core and lab based sub that help stud gain tech skill. The plan is in line with uni rules and gives base for both jobs and high study. The exam sys has two mid sem tests and one end sem exam for each sem. Marks are split with near 40% from mid tests, assign and lab work and 60% from the end sem exam. The exams are of mid to high level and most stud who keep up with class and lab work are able to pass. A small set of stud may fail in tough sub but they can clear them in sup exams. The course plan is quite apt for eng study and helps build both theory know how and real use skill.

Curriculum needs to focus on what you can do with information rather than the information itself. I wouldnâ€™t eliminate content, without background we cannot form good, rational, and educated conclusions. We are still giving timed tests in math facts! I know thatâ€™s traditional, but it just isnâ€™t as important. My era had no calculators and we needed to be proficient at paper and pencil solutions, but now, perfect calculations are available to everyone. We should focus on what kinds of problems can be solved with mathematics. Spelling is equally less important. Vocabulary should be stressed. My sister years ago was taking a test for a personal assistant position where she was asked to type a large number of words per minute error free. She was laughing at such a test. She said no one does that anymore, they just type then run spell check. Technology is changing our NEEDS opinion is that curriculum should reflect our reality. No one gets paid to solve problems that have already been solved. Students should be exposed to solving problems they have not seen before but that can be solve with the content they have learned in class. This will help them develop problem solving strategies they will be able to use the rest of their lives

The course was quite comprehensive and relevant. They covered all the fields of life sciences in depth- molecular biology, recombinant DNA technology (RDT), microbiology, biostatistics, instrumentation, and immunology, among others. The combination of both foundational and applied topics was the whole reason I chose the course at SVSU. In terms of preparationfor the real world applications, the curriculum does a great job in building clarity and founding strong concepts. For students aiming to prepare for GATE, NET, and higher research projects to delve into, the syllabus provides a strong foundation. Regarding improvements, the lab work could be more one-on-one rather than a bulk implementation, and more hands-on experience needs to be added to the course in comparison to the theoretical knowledge. Speaking of the examination pattern, it works in semesters, with the first one occurring in December, then around April, and the last one around June-July. Other than that, there are internal tests held bimonthly and project submissions before the examination session.
